entry.title: Dump When Checking Faulty RAP Object Based On Union CDS-View (OnPremise 2023 SP01)
entry.content:
Dear Community,

Recently I found a bug when checking/activating a RAP object which is based on a union CDS-View and has an error across multiple artifacts. I would like to describe and summarize this for reporting and to save other people’s time during analysis.

Initial Situation

Let's say I want to consolidate different findings from our beloved sflight. For this purpose, I created the following union view:

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [ #NONE ]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Unifies different validation findings'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

define root view entity ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION
  as select from ZI_DELAYED_FLIGHTS

{
  key Carrid,
  key Connid,
  key Fldate,

      ValidationResult,
      ValidationCategory,
      DelayInMinutes,
      -1                  as MaxPassengers,
      -1                  as BookedPassengers
}

union
  select from ZI_OVERBOOKED_FLIGHTS

{
  key Carrid,
  key Connid,
  key Fldate,

      ValidationResult,
      ValidationCategory,
      -1                  as DelayInMinutes,
      MaxPassengers,
      BookedPassengers
}

Based on that union, I want to create a simple Fiori Elements app in which the user can see all the different findings and is able to create comments via an action. For the action we create the following Behavior Definition:

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_i_flight_findings_union unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION

lock master
authorization master ( instance )

{
  action addComment parameter char200;
}

 I decided to go for an unmanaged implementation, because unions do not easily support CRUD operations (and they are not needed for the business case yet). The handler class in the class pool looks like this:

CLASS lhc_ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR zi_flight_findings_union RESULT result.

    METHODS addComment FOR MODIFY
IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION zi_flight_findings_union~addComment.

    METHODS read FOR READ
      IMPORTING keys FOR READ zi_flight_findings_union RESULT result.
ENDCLASS.


CLASS lhc_ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_instance_authorizations.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD addComment.
    "Create comment by EML ....
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD read.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Everything works, everything can be activated. But now silly me wants to save additional data from our ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION object. Reading with EML isn't possible yet, because read isn't implemented. To avoid implementing the read method myself, I got the idea to change the Behavior Definition to managed with unmanaged save (would this even work?):

managed with unmanaged save implementation in class zbp_i_flight_findings_union unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION
lock master
authorization master ( instance )
{
  action addComment parameter char200;
}

Now I introduced some inconsistencies, because the handler still contains the read method.
But these errors are hidden now.

There is no warning inside the behavior definition, but activating leads to the following error:

 

By checking ST22 we can see that we get for every syntax check and every activation the following dump:

 

If we don't know the mistake we made inside the behavior definition and its implementation we have no indication of what went wrong.

How this dump happens

The dump happens inside the Class CL_CSP_MD_ANNOTATION_READER in method create_by_metadata:

    lo_instance->mt_element_annotation = CORRESPONDING #( is_cds_annotations_md-elements MAPPING element_name     = elementname
                                                                                                 annotation_name  = annoname
                                                                                                 annotation_value = value ).

This statement tries to write values from is_cds_annotations_md-elements to lo_instance->mt_element_annotation.  That table expects a unique key with annotation_name and element_name.

 

But this uniqueness is not given:

 

The reason is in table DDFIELDANNO in which the assigned annotations are stored. The annotation ABAPCATALOG.INTERNAL.ISMANDT is stored twice, because of our union: 

Workaround

Caution: Be careful when working in this area, especially when debugging or activating the object. I cannot guarantee that it won’t lead to inconsistencies or other issues in your system. You do everything at your own risk.

At the end of method IF_CSP_WRP_DD_ANNOTATION~GET_ANNOS_MASS in class CL_CSP_WRP_DD_ANNOTATION I placed a Breakpoint, triggered the check (not the activation!) and was able to remove the duplicate line. After that, I was able to get the real finding:

"Class can't be analyzed during BDEF implementation check. Inconsistencies or errors exist: "ZI_FLIGHT_FINDINGS_UNION" has the implementation type "managed", hence the implementation type "READ" is superfluous."

Conclusion

With this workaround it's possible to avoid the dump and retrieve the actual finding.

To fix the dump, the duplicate entry must be prevented. I assume that either the selection (method _GET_DRCT_ANNS_4_ENTT_ELMNTS_M in class CL_DD_DDL_ANNOTATION_SERVICE) or the merging of the annotations (method MERGE_ENTITIES_ELMNTS_ANNOS in class CL_DD_DDL_ANNOTATION_MERGER) might be the relevant place to fix it.

I'm also creating an incident and keep you updated. If it's already fixed after 2023 SP01, please let me know.

Thank you for reading and best regards
Marc

entry.title: Fiori List Report: The "Process All" Dilemma — Parsing OData $filter in ABAP
entry.content:
The Problem

This scenario is more common than you might think.

In the old SAP GUI world, ALV lists frequently had buttons like "Execute All" or "Process All" defined in the GUI Status, or users could simply "Select All" rows and trigger an action. The entire dataset was right there in memory — stateful, accessible, simple.

Unfortunately, the same approach is still often requested in Fiori Elements List Reports. The reason? There's still a widespread difficulty in understanding the fundamental difference between stateful and stateless, between a classic ALV list and a Fiori List Report.

When we try to explain that there's an OData protocol with pagination behind the scenes, that "Select All" doesn't truly select all rows, or that an "Execute All" action has no knowledge of which records to process — unlike in ALV where the entire internal table was available — we can often steer the design away from this pattern. But sometimes, we can't.

The Fiori warning message helps users understand that "Select All" is only partial — it's no longer the same as it was with ALV lists.

There are legitimate use cases where, after filtering a dataset, reviewing it, validating it, the user needs to "confirm" an action or a status change to be applied to that specific result set they're currently looking at.

I won't go into the details of how the action should be implemented in ABAP — there could be timeout concerns, and often these actions are executed via background tasks or jobs. I want to focus specifically on the approach for capturing the "snapshot" of what the user has filtered.

As we said, "Select All" is not a viable option. In the modern world we have pagination, and we can't expect the user to scroll all the way to the bottom of the list. Retrieving all row identifiers from the frontend is simply not feasible.

Our Approach

The best solution we've implemented (together with @SimoneLicciardi ) is to pass the applied filter criteria back to a Static Action — essentially the same filters that were active when the user pressed "Go".

We can define an Abstract Entity as the import parameter for the RAP action. If the action dialog already has other parameters, this is simply an additional hidden parameter (@UI.hidden: true) that we can call FilterString.

define abstract entity ZA_ProcessAllParams {
  @UI.hidden: true
  FilterString : abap.string(4000);
  // ... other action parameters if needed
}

The Fiori Elements Extension

On the Fiori Elements List Report side, we need custom action logic that does two things when the user triggers the action: capture the current $filter string and inject it into the hidden parameter.

This can be achieved through a custom action defined in the manifest.json (or via a controller extension, depending on your app setup). 

The _getFilterString method extracts the $filter from the current table row binding — this is exactly the same filter the framework sent to the backend when the user pressed "Go".

function _getFilterString(oView) {
    // Table ID follows Fiori Elements convention: 
    // "<appId>::<ListReportPage>--fe::table::<EntitySet>::LineItem"
    var oTable = oView.byId("<your-table-control-id>");
    if (oTable) {
        var oRowBinding = oTable.getRowBinding();
        if (oRowBinding) {
            var sPath = oRowBinding.getDownloadUrl();
            if (sPath) {
                var iFilterStart = sPath.indexOf("$filter=");
                if (iFilterStart > -1) {
                    var sFilter = sPath.substring(iFilterStart + 8);
                    var iNextParam = sFilter.indexOf("&");
                    if (iNextParam > -1) {
                        sFilter = sFilter.substring(0, iNextParam);
                    }
                    return decodeURIComponent(sFilter);
                }
            }
        }
    }
    return "";
}

Then in the action handler, we invoke the static action and pass the $filter string as the hidden parameter:

onPressExecuteAll: function () {
    var oView = this.editFlow.getView();
    var sActionName = "<your action bound context>";
    var sFilterString = _getFilterString(oView);

    var oInvokeParams = {
        controlId: "<your-table-control-id>",
        bStaticAction: true,
        invocationGrouping: "Isolated",
        parameterValues: [{
            name: "FilterString",
            value: sFilterString
        }]
    };

    this.editFlow.invokeAction(sActionName, oInvokeParams)
        .then(function (oResult) {
            // handle success
        }).catch(function (oError) {
            console.error("Action error:", oError);
        });
}

The key point: the parameterValues array injects the filter string into the hidden FilterString field of the Abstract Entity. The user never sees it — the action dialog shows only the visible parameters (if any), while the filter criteria travel silently to the backend.

Note:  this was tested on S/4HANA 2023 with OData V4 and SAPUI5 1.120.

The ABAP Parser

On the backend, the action handler receives the $filter string and needs to parse it into ABAP range tables to re-execute the query with the same criteria.

We looked for standard solutions — for example, the method ODATA_FILTER2SELECT_OPTION of class CL_CLB2_TOOLS — but it only supports OData V2.

So we built a custom reusable class: ZCL_ODATA_FILTER_PARSER, which handles both V2 and V4 syntax and converts the $filter string directly into ABAP range tables.

DATA(lo_parser) = NEW zcl_odata_filter_parser(
  iv_filter  = lv_filter_string
  iv_version = zcl_odata_filter_parser=>gc_version-v4
).

DATA(lt_r_plant) = lo_parser->get_range( 'Plant' ).
DATA(lt_r_mat)   = lo_parser->get_range( 'Material' ).
DATA(lt_r_date)  = lo_parser->get_range( 'DeliveryDate' ).

The class is open source, includes ABAP Unit tests  and is installable via abapGit. You can find the  usage examples in the repository:

https://github.com/alespad/abap-odata-filter-parser 

This is how we approached solving this problem. If you know of better approaches, closer to standard functionality, we'd love to hear about them — please share in the comments!

entry.title: Custom Business Configurations (F4579): Define default grouping and sorting
entry.content:
Introduction

The Custom Business Configurations (CUBCO) app serves as an entry point to the Business Configuration Maintenance Object (SMBC) provided by custom applications or partners.

This blog explains how to define the default grouping and sorting for the tables of the business configuration object.
The basic procedure is already explained here, but we apply it directly to the result of the ADT wizard.

This blog is relevant for

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 
  • SAP BTP ABAP environment 
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 

Further reading:

  • Related blog posts
  • Learn how you can use ABAP technology to develop innovative applications and business solutions across SAP’s portfolio on SAP Learning Site.

Example scenario

Company codes are defined in the configuration table ZDEMO_CC. In the CUBCO app, the rows are to be grouped and sorted by country.

@EndUserText.label : 'Company Code'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zdemo_cc {
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key company_code      : abap.numc(4) not null;
  country               : land1;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
}

Define presentation variant

First you use the ADT wizard to generate a Business Configuration Maintenance Object for this table.

In the generated CDS view ZI_COMPANYCODE_S of the Singleton entity, you change the facet type from #LINEITEM_REFERENCE to #PRESENTATIONVARIANT_REFERENCE and add the targetQualifier attribute:

  .facet: [ {
    id: 'ZI_CompanyCode', 
    purpose: #STANDARD, 
    type: #PRESENTATIONVARIANT_REFERENCE, 
    targetQualifier: 'myVariant',
    label: 'Company Codes', 
    position: 1 , 
    targetElement: '_CompanyCode'
  } ]
  .lineItem: [ {
    position: 1 
  } ]
  key 1 as SingletonID,

In the metadata extension ZI_COMPANYCODE of the company code entity, add the following presentation variant:

.presentationVariant: [
  {
    qualifier: 'myVariant',
    sortOrder: [
      {
        by: 'Country',
        direction: #ASC
      }
    ],
    groupBy: [ 'Country' ],
    visualizations: [{type: #AS_LINEITEM}]
  }
]
annotate view ZI_CompanyCode with

Finally, open the generated SMBC object ZCOMPANYCODE in ADT and open the table settings section. Change the table type attribute from Grid Table to Responsive Table because grouping is not supported for grid tables. If you only need the default sorting, you can continue to use the grid table.

entry.title: Our 2026 Roadmap for Joule for Developers ABAP AI capabilities
entry.content:
Sapphire 2026 is approaching quickly and as usual it will bring lots of new announcements. So now is the right point in time to recap what has been announced already until Q2/2026 and prepare for the ABAP AI sessions.

The ABAP Platform AI (covering both SAP BTP ABAP Environment and SAP S/4HANA) transitions from a set of independent AI skills to full-scale Agentic AI. The roadmap focuses heavily on increasing developer productivity via agents in Joule for Developers ABAP (J4D ABAP) and the modernization of ABAP development tools.

The key benefits for customers and partners of the roadmap until Q2/2026 are:

  1. ABAP development tools with AI Agents in VS Code with ABAP MCP Server.
  2. ABAP AI for lower releases of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (i.e. 2021, 2022, 2023 and 2025).
  3. Simplified customer on-boarding of ABAP AI via SAP for Me and Central Business Configuration.
  4. Faster innovation with Side-by-Side consumption of SAP and 3rd party Agentic IDEs and LLMs
    (e.g. Microsoft, Amazon, IBM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral…)
 

The biggest shift in 2026 will be the move toward Agentic AI, where the existing ABAP AI skills doesn't just explain, complete or generate code (e.g. RAP, CDS, Tests) independently but acts as an autonomous collaborator. This includes built-in ABAP MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. The introduction of VS Code as IDE can only be a start as the depth and breath of Eclipse which we achieved in so many years cannot be reached with one or two steps in VS Code. It'll be a journey which starts with first providing End-to-end UI services development with agents.

The new ABAP AI side-by-side architecture is designed to provide a scalable and future‑proof foundation for ABAP AI capabilities across heterogeneous SAP landscapes. It enables customers to consume ABAP AI services side‑by‑side, independent of the underlying S/4HANA release, ensuring access even for lower‑release Private Edition systems. 

A core benefit of the architecture is also its simplified enablement model through SAP for Me and Central Business Configuration, reducing on-boarding effort and ensuring consistent lifecycle management across environments. 
In addition, the architecture allows us to accelerate innovation speed by allowing to leverage SAP‑provided ABAP AI models alongside leading third‑party LLMs (e.g., Microsoft, Amazon, IBM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral) more independently from releases. This flexibility ensures rapid adoption of new capabilities without dependency on individual release cycles.

If you missed the highlights we delivered in Q1/2026 have a look into what Olga Dolinskaja shared about ABAP AI ATC finding explain and custom code explain and proposals. Although this will be a focus also for our future roadmap we will also not stop investing into AI app generators like the Embedded Analytics model generator. 

 
Here you can learn more:
  1. SAP Joule for Developers (J4D) Expands to Private Cloud: Accelerating ABAP Innovation and Transformation
  2. Your Roadmap for getting started with ABAP AI
  3. ABAP AI Revolution Accelerates: The ABAP Developer Who Built Enterprise Apps in Minutes
  4. Joule for Developers and ABAP AI capabilities are coming to SAP S/4HANA Private Edition 2021, 2022, and 2023
  5. Public Roadmap: SAP Help / SAP Roadmap Explorer
  6. Help & Guides
    1. Step-by-step guide how to activate SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
    2. Joule for Developers (J4D) for S/4HANA Public Cloud Setup Guide
    3. SAP ABAP AI Discovery Center
    4. SAP BTP Discovery Center (Free Tier Services)
    5. Custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
    6. YouTube playlist for Joule for Developers (J4D)
    7. Joule for Developers Help (J4D)
    8. Build SAP Fiori Apps with ABAP Cloud and SAP Joule for developers (RAP120) 
    9. ADT Eclipse AI Capabilities Help
    10. ABAP AI SDK Help
    11. ABAP-1 Help

More high-level insights about Joule for Developers/ABAP AI and ABAP platform can be found here . Here you find more on ABAP- 1,

entry.title: The Agentic Revolution Is Here — And Your ABAP Code Is the Foundation
entry.content:
Three Waves in Two Years

To understand where we are, you need to understand the three inflection points Jensen described. They happened faster than anyone expected.

Wave 1: Generative AI (2022–2023)

ChatGPT changed computing from retrieval-based to generative. Instead of a database returning a record, a model generates an answer. Jensen put it bluntly: "Computing used to be retrieval-based. Now it's generative." The architecture implications of that sentence are enormous and still rippling through every layer of enterprise software.

Wave 2: Reasoning AI (2024)

OpenAI's o1 model introduced something that made generative AI trustworthy: the ability to think. To reflect. To decompose a problem it couldn't solve into steps it could. To ground itself on facts before answering. Reasoning AI made large language models genuinely useful for complex, multi-step work — not just creative writing and summarisation.

Wave 3: Agentic AI (2025–2026)

This is the one that changes your job. Jensen cited Claude Code — the very AI assistant many of us now use daily — as the breakthrough moment:

"Claude Code has revolutionised software engineering. There is not one software engineer at NVIDIA today who is not assisted by an AI coding agent."

The shift is fundamental. With generative AI, you ask a question. With agentic AI, you give a task. The agent reads your files, writes code, compiles it, tests it, finds the failures, fixes them, and iterates — autonomously, in a loop, until the work is done or it needs your input.

Computing demand, Jensen said, has grown one million times in the last two years. That is not a typo. The explosion is driven by agentic inference: every time an agent thinks, plans, reads a file, calls a tool, or generates an output, it burns tokens.

GTC 2026 Keynote — Jensen Huang's enterprise agentic architectures slide (02:02:57). SAP is bottom-left. Each diagram is a production blueprint, not a roadmap.

 

What Jensen Showed at 02:02:57

At just over two hours into the keynote, Jensen showed a slide he called a renaissance. Each of the twelve architecture diagrams was a real, production blueprint. Not a roadmap. Not a vision. Each company had already rebuilt its product around AI agents.

"This is a reinvention. A renaissance of enterprise IT. Every single SaaS company will become an Agentic as a Service company. No question about it."

He compared it to three previous platform shifts:

  • When Linux arrived → every company needed a Linux strategy
  • When HTML/HTTP arrived → every company needed a web strategy
  • When Kubernetes arrived → every company needed a cloud-native strategy
  • Now Now OpenClaw (the open-source agentic OS) has arrived → every company needs an agentic strategy

NemoClaw and SAP: A Security Partnership

Jensen announced NemoClaw as NVIDIA's enterprise-hardened version of OpenClaw. To understand where it fits in the SAP picture, it helps to be precise about what it is — and what it is not.

OpenClaw is, in Jensen's words, "the operating system of agentic computers." It handles task decomposition, sub-agent spawning, tool calling, file access, cron scheduling, multi-modal I/O, and LLM orchestration. It reached more GitHub stars in a few weeks than Linux achieved in thirty years.

The enterprise problem with a system that can "access sensitive information, execute code, and communicate externally" is obvious. You cannot deploy raw OpenClaw in an SAP landscape and have it reach your vendor master, financial postings, and HR records without controls.

NemoClaw solves this by adding three security layers on top of OpenClaw:

Layer

What It Does

OpenShell

Sandboxes agent execution; controls what it can read, write, and call

Policy Engine

Enforces the enterprise's own business rules

Network Guardrail
+ Privacy Router

Prevents data leaving the enterprise boundary

 

Important clarification: NemoClaw is not the foundation SAP Joule is built on. Joule is SAP's own product, with its own architecture, models, and AI infrastructure running on BTP and SAP AI Core. NemoClaw is NVIDIA technology that SAP — as a launch enterprise partner — is integrating as an optional security and governance layer within BTP, tied specifically to Joule Studio. It is available for customers who require the strongest possible compliance controls when agents interact with sensitive ERP, financial, or supply chain data.

The architecture looks like this:

The bottom line for ABAP developers remains unchanged regardless of which security envelope is in play: when an agent needs to do something in SAP — read a purchase order, create a vendor, trigger a workflow, query a cost centre — it calls your ABAP.

Your BAPIs. Your CDS views. Your OData services.

 

What This Means in Practice

Let me make this concrete with an example that exists today.

A procurement manager asks Joule: "Which of our top twenty suppliers have delivery performance below 90% this quarter, and what is the total open invoice value against them?"

In the pre-agentic world, someone writes a report in SE38, schedules it, exports to Excel, and manually cross-references two tables. Elapsed time: hours, or days if it hits a queue.

In the agentic world, Joule:

  1. Decomposes the question into sub-tasks
  2. Calls a procurement agent → queries I_SupplierDeliveryPerformance CDS view via OData
  3. Calls a finance agent → queries I_SupplierOpenItemMD CDS view via OData
  4. Synthesises both results
  5. Returns a structured answer in seconds

The entire chain runs through NemoClaw's policy engine and your ABAP authorization framework. The agent's service user has only the authorizations you have granted. Every CDS view it accesses must have the proper access control annotation and a corresponding DCL.

 

The Four Things Your ABAP Must Get Right
If agents are going to call your code — and they are — four things matter more than they ever did before.


1. Clean, Annotated CDS Views


CDS views are the primary structured data interface for AI agents in SAP. An agent does not navigate transaction codes. It calls OData endpoints backed by CDS. If your CDS views lack semantic annotations, have incomplete access control, or expose data structures that predate any logical data model, they become a liability.The minimum bar for agent-ready CDS:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'Supplier Delivery Performance'
@VDM.viewType: #CONSUMPTION
@OData.publish: true
define view entity I_SupplierDeliveryPerformance
  as select from ...
{
  @Semantics.businessPartner.role: #SUPPLIER
  SupplierID,

  @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure: 'DeliveryUnit'
  ConfirmedDeliveryQty,

  @Semantics.percentage
  OnTimeDeliveryRate
}

Semantic annotations tell the LLM what each field means. Without them, the agent is guessing from column names. With them, it can reason correctly.

 

2. Authority Checks on Every Agent-Callable Interface


NemoClaw's policy engine delegates final security enforcement to ABAP. This is not a weakness — it is the correct architecture. The LLM should not be trusted with authorization decisions. ABAP should.
Every function module, BAPI, and method that an agent can call must perform an explicit authority check:

METHOD get_supplier_invoice_data.
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_LFA1_BUK'
    ID 'BUKRS' FIELD iv_company_code
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_sy_authorization_check
      EXPORTING
        textid = cx_sy_authorization_check=>no_authorization.
  ENDIF.

  " ... proceed with data retrieval
ENDMETHOD.

An agent running as a service user with overly broad authorizations is an insider threat waiting to happen. Lock it down at the ABAP layer.

3. Machine-Speed Performance


A human clicks a button every thirty seconds. An agent may call your BAPI ten thousand times per hour. An interface that performs acceptably under human load may collapse completely under agent load.
Review your agent-callable interfaces against these criteria:
• No SELECT * in performance-critical paths — agents fetch data in loops, field-level selects only.
• Buffer vendor master, material master, and configuration data where appropriate.
• Avoid dialogue modules and unnecessary commits in agent-exposed interfaces — agents expect atomic, stateless calls.
• Consider ABAP Cloud compatibility — if you are on S/4HANA, agent integrations will increasingly come via BTP.


4. Reliable, Structured Error Returns


Agents chain multiple tool calls into a single business process. If step three of a five-step chain fails silently — no exception raised, just a blank result set — the agent may proceed with corrupted state and complete a transaction based on missing data.
Your interfaces need to fail loudly, clearly, and catchably:

METHOD create_purchase_requisition.
TRY.
" ... BAPI call
IF lt_return[] IS NOT INITIAL.
LOOP AT lt_return INTO ls_return
WHERE type = 'E' OR type = 'A'.
RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_pr_creation_failed
EXPORTING
message = ls_return-message
message_id = ls_return-id
number = ls_return-number.
ENDLOOP.
ENDIF.
CATCH cx_sy_authorization_check INTO DATA(lx_auth).
RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_pr_creation_failed
EXPORTING message = 'Authorization check failed'.
ENDTRY.
ENDMETHOD.

A well-structured exception gives the orchestration layer the information it needs to retry, compensate, or escalate. A dump gives it nothing.

 

Physical AI: Why EWM and IBP Developers Should Pay Close Attention

Jensen spent a significant portion of the keynote on physical AI — robots, autonomous vehicles, digital twins. At first glance this might seem distant from ABAP.  It is not.

He announced partnerships with ABB, KUKA, Universal Robots, BYD, Hyundai, Nissan, and Geely — collectively representing the automation of manufacturing floors and logistics networks at a scale never seen before. These physical systems will generate transactions. Inventory movements, goods receipts, production confirmations, delivery completions — triggered by machines at machine speed, not by warehouse workers scanning barcodes.

If you work in SAP Extended Warehouse Management, SAP Integrated Business Planning, or SAP Manufacturing Integration and Intelligence, the interfaces you build today will be called by robots tomorrow. The same four principles apply — and the performance requirement becomes even more acute.

 

Your Learning Roadmap

The field is moving fast. But not everything needs to be learned at once. Here is how I would prioritise it.

Start Now — Zero Extra Investment Required

  • AI tools today. Use AI coding tools on real ABAP work — every unit test, every code review, every CDS view.
  • Learn what SAP Joule can already do. Log into your BTP account and explore the Joule capabilities available today.
  • Audit your CDS views. Run a quick check across your landscape for CDS views with @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED — these are potential agent-accessible data leaks.

Next Three to Six Months

  • ABAP RAP (RESTful Application Programming Model). If you have been deferring it, stop. RAP is the model that produces the OData V4 services that Joule agents consume.
  • SAP AI Core and SAP BTP basics. You do not need to become a BTP developer. Understand the platform well enough to design ABAP interfaces that integrate cleanly with it.
  • Python literacy. Not fluency — literacy. Most AI tooling and agent orchestration frameworks are Python-first. Being able to read a Python agent script is now a professional responsibility.

Strategic Awareness

  • OpenClaw and MCP (Model Context Protocol). OpenClaw is the open-source agent OS. MCP is the protocol that defines how agents discover and call tools. SAP Joule implements MCP under the hood.
  • HANA Vector Engine. HANA Cloud's vector engine brings unstructured data (PDFs, emails, contracts) directly into your SAP data layer.

 

The Part Nobody Is Saying Loudly Enough

Jensen made a prediction that deserves more attention than it received.

In the near future, every engineer at NVIDIA will receive an annual token budget — roughly equivalent to half their salary in additional compensation — because AI-amplified engineers are demonstrably ten times more productive. Engineers are already asking: "How many tokens come with this job?"

Here is the part that matters for us specifically:

AI tokens without SAP domain expertise produce plausible-looking but wrong ABAP. An LLM that does not understand the difference between a financial posting document and a parked document will generate code that compiles, passes basic tests, and silently corrupts your general ledger. It will call RFC_READ_TABLE in production because it found an example online. It will bypass the credit check in a sales order because it did not know the check existed.

Your twenty years of knowing where the bodies are buried in SAP — that is not a liability in an AI-augmented world. It is the irreplaceable ingredient that makes the AI's output correct and trustworthy.

The developers who will thrive are not the ones who resist this shift. And they are not the ones who hand everything to an LLM and hope for the best. They are the ones who treat AI as a powerful amplifier of their own judgment — who know enough to direct it well, verify its output, and design the systems it integrates with.

That combination — deep SAP domain knowledge plus AI tool fluency — is genuinely rare right now. The window to build it is open. It will not stay open long.

 

Wrapping Up

GTC 2026 was not a technology conference for GPU enthusiasts. It was a signal that the enterprise software industry has passed an inflection point. Jensen's slide showing SAP, Salesforce, ServiceNow, and Siemens side by side — each with a production agentic architecture — was not a prediction of what is coming. It was a photograph of what has already happened.

SAP has placed its bet. Joule is real, BTP is the platform, and the structured data of your SAP landscape — managed, modelled, and secured by ABAP code — is what gives every agent running on top of it the ground truth it needs to be useful.

Action Items

✓  Write clean CDS views.

✓  Lock down your authority checks.

✓  Tune for machine-speed consumption.

✓  Raise clear exceptions.

✓  Learn RAP.

✓  Open Claude Code.

  

The revolution is not coming for your job. It is coming for the parts of your job that should have been automated years ago. What remains — and what becomes more valuable — is everything that requires judgment, domain knowledge, and the ability to see when the AI is confidently wrong.

That is the ABAP developer of 2026.

entry.title: Moving from Hub Gateway to Direct S/4HANA OData V4: A Customized Reusable RAP Processing Framework
entry.content:
In SAP landscapes using a Gateway Hub architecture, OData services are typically exposed through a separate Gateway system, with data processing handled in the backend S/4HANA system via RFC calls. While this architecture has been widely used, it introduces additional system dependencies and layers of communication.

With the adoption of ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) and OData V4, services can now be exposed directly from S/4HANA, allowing developers to simplify the architecture and implement more structured service processing patterns.

In this article, I share a framework concept I have used in my developments to organize how requests to RAP Custom Entities are handled. The approach introduces a reusable processing structure for request handling, data extraction, transformation, and response construction.

This concept is intended to improve maintainability, extensibility, and consistency when building RAP-based services, using existing ABAP objects.

End-to-End Implementation

The framework is implemented as a base processing class that contains the common request handling logic such as entity configuration loading, request context extraction, data transformation, and response construction.

This class is not intended to be used directly. Instead, it is designed to be subclassed for each specific service implementation, where entity-specific logic such as data extraction and transformations can be implemented.

To better understand how the framework works, let's walk through the runtime execution flow when a client calls the OData V4 service.

Custom Entity Implementation

@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Order Details (OData V4)'
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true

@ObjectModel.query: {
  implementedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_V4_SALESORDER_QUERY'
}

define root custom entity ZCE_SalesOrderDetails
{
  key SalesOrder        : vbeln;
  key SalesOrderItem    : posnr;
      Material          : matnr;
      Customer          : kunnr;
      OrderQuantity     : abap.quan(13,3);
      OrderUnit         : meins;
      NetPrice          : abap.curr(15,2);
      Currency          : waers;
      CreatedBy         : syuname;
      CreatedOn         : abap.dats;
      DeliveryDate      : abap.dats;

      _ScheduleLines : composition [0..*] of ZCE_SalesOrderSchedule;
}

 

Using the Framework Class as a Superclass

The class ZSV00CL_CE_V2TOV4_CONVERSION acts as a generic processing framework for RAP Custom Entities. It provides reusable logic for handling the complete request lifecycle, including request handling, data retrieval orchestration, payload extraction, entity transformation, and response construction.

To use this framework for a specific business entity, a child class must be created that inherits from the framework class. For example: 

ZCL_V4_SALESORDER_QUERY
 
This class is defined as:
CLASS zcl_v4_salesorder_query DEFINITION
PUBLIC
INHERITING FROM zsv00cl_ce_v2tov4_conversion
FINAL
CREATE PUBLIC.

By inheriting from the framework class, the child class automatically gains access to the entire processing pipeline implemented in the superclass, including the implementation of the RAP interface method:

 
IF_RAP_QUERY_PROVIDER~SELECT

This means the child class does not need to reimplement the standard request processing logic.

RAP Custom Entity Processing Framework – Method Responsibilities

 

MethodLayerWhen It Is CalledPurposeKey Actions

MethodLayerWhen its calledPurposeKey Actions
IF_RAP_QUERY_PROVIDER~SELECTRAP Entry LayerTriggered when OData V4 service is calledEntry point for RAP query processingChecks if data is requested, loads entity configurations, and starts request handling
LOAD_ENTITY_CONFIGURATIONSConfiguration LayerImmediately after SELECTLoads metadata configuration for entitiesDefines entity mapping, source entity set, source/target types, and field mappings
HANDLE_DATA_REQUESTProcessing ControllerAfter configurations are loadedMain orchestrator for request executionCalls context extraction, determines entity type, and triggers fetch + transformation logic
GET_REQUEST_CONTEXTContext LayerEarly in request handlingExtracts request parameters from RAP requestReads filters, paging info, entity name, and key values
IS_ROOT_ENTITYRouting LayerAfter context extractionDetermines whether request is root or child entityControls whether data must be fetched or extracted from existing payload
HANDLE_DATA_FETCHData Retrieval LayerWhen root entity is requestedRetrieves data from backend sourcesBuilds request keys, identifies main source entity set, and triggers backend retrieval (RFC / OData V2 / API)
CALL_RFC_FOR_POST (optional)Integration LayerDuring data fetch if backend logic neededCalls external logic such as RFC or APIUsed for backend system communication
EXTRACT_DATAPayload LayerAfter backend data is retrievedExtracts entity-specific data from payload containerDynamically unwraps source entity data into ABAP structures/tables
HANDLE_ENTITY_CONVERSIONS_GETTransformation ControllerAfter data extractionControls transformation processDetermines if source data is structure or table and calls appropriate conversion method
TRANSFORM_STRUCTURE_DATATransformation LayerWhen source payload is a structureConverts single record structure from V2 → V4Applies field mapping and conversion exits
TRANSFORM_TABLE_DATATransformation LayerWhen source payload is a tableConverts multiple records from V2 → V4Iterates over rows and applies field mapping
CONVERSION_EXITBusiness Logic LayerDuring transformationApplies custom logic for specific fieldsHandles timestamp conversion, code mapping, calculated fields
SET_RESPONSE_DATAResponse LayerFinal step before returning resultSends processed data back to RAP frameworkSets data and total record count in response object

 

High-Level Runtime Flow

 

Detailed Method Execution Sequence

Below is the exact order of method execution when the OData V4 service is called.

Step 1 — RAP Query Trigger

When a consumer calls the Custom Entity OData V4 service, RAP invokes :

METHOD if_rap_query_provider~select.

 Example implementation:

METHOD if_rap_query_provider~select.

  IF io_request->is_data_requested( ).

    load_entity_configurations( ).

    handle_data_request(
      ii_request  = io_request
      ii_response = io_response ).

  ENDIF.

ENDMETHOD.

What happens here?

  1. RAP checks if data is requested.
  2. Entity mappings are loaded.
  3. The main processing method is called.

Step 2 - Loading Configurations

The method initializes the processing setup for the requested entity. It determines the entity being requested and loads the relevant configuration that defines how the framework should retrieve, process, and transform the data.

This step allows the framework to dynamically control entity-specific logic while keeping the main request handling flow generic and reusable.

Method: 

LOAD_ENTITY_CONFIGURATIONS

Example:

gt_entity_config = VALUE #( ( entity_id       = 'oDataV4 Entity 1 '
                              entity_set      = 'oDataV2 Entity Set'
                              src_type        = 'Table Type for oDataV2 Entity 1'
                              trg_type        = 'Table Type for oDataV4 Entity 1'
                              src_type_structure = abap_true
                              src_is_entity_set = abap_true
                              field_map       = VALUE ty_abap_map_tab(  ( src_field = 'oData V2 Field 1'  trg_field = 'oData V4 Field 1' )
                                                                        ( src_field = 'oData V2 Field 2'  trg_field = 'oData V4 Field 2'  ) ) )
                            ( entity_id       = 'oDataV4 Entity 2 '
                              entity_set      = 'oDataV2 Entity Set'
                              src_type        = 'Table Type for oDataV2 Entity 2'
                              trg_type        = 'Table Type for oDataV4 Entity 2'
                              src_type_structure = abap_true
                              src_is_entity_set = abap_true
                              field_map       = VALUE ty_abap_map_tab( ( src_field = 'oData V2 Field 1' trg_field = 'oData V4 Field 1' )
                                                                       ( src_field = 'oData V2 Field 2' trg_field = 'oData V4 Field 2' ) ) ) .

Step 2 — Request Context Extraction

The framework extracts important request parameters.

Method called:

GET_REQUEST_CONTEXT

Example:

DATA(ls_context) = get_request_context( ii_request ).
The context contains:
  • Filters
  • Page size
  • Entity ID
  • Key values
Structure example:
Entity ID
Filter Conditions
Paging Information
Keys

 

Step 3 — Identify Root Entity

The framework checks whether the request is for a root entity or a child entity. You can write your own logic here on how the root can be determined. 

Method: 

IS_ROOT_ENTITY
Why is this needed?

Because root entities trigger the initial data retrieval, while child entities use already available payload data.

 

Step 4 — Fetch Source Data

If the request is for the root entity, the following method is executed:

HANDLE_DATA_FETCH

 Example implementation:

METHOD handle_data_fetch.

  build_keys(
    iv_fieldname = 'FIELD_ID'
    iv_value     = 'FIELD_VALUE' ).

  is_main_source_entity_set( ).

ENDMETHOD.
 Responsibilities of this method:
  • Build request keys
  • Determine the main source entity set
  • Trigger backend data retrieval (custom implementation)

    This method is typically redefined in subclasses.

Step 5 — Convert Entity Data

Next, the framework converts the source payload to the target entity.

Method:

HANDLE_ENTITY_CONVERSIONS_GET

This method performs the main transformation workflow.

Example: 
    FIELD-SYMBOLS: <lfs_t_output_data> TYPE STANDARD TABLE.
    FIELD-SYMBOLS: <lfs_t_source_data> TYPE STANDARD TABLE.
    FIELD-SYMBOLS: <lfs_source_data> TYPE any.
    FIELD-SYMBOLS: <lfs_output_data> TYPE any.
    DATA: lr_data TYPE REF TO data.

    " Extract source data for the  entity
    DATA(lr_child_data) = extract_data( is_context-entity_id ).
    CHECK lr_child_data IS BOUND.

    READ TABLE gt_entity_config INTO DATA(ls_entity_config) WITH KEY entity_id          = is_context-entity_id
                                                                     src_is_entity_set  = mv_main_source_entity_set.
    CHECK sy-subrc = 0.

    IF ls_entity_config-src_type_structure = abap_true.
      ASSIGN lr_child_data->* TO <lfs_source_data>.
    ELSE.
      ASSIGN lr_child_data->* TO <lfs_t_source_data>.
    ENDIF.

    DATA(lr_transformed_data) = COND #( WHEN ls_entity_config-src_type_structure = abap_true AND <lfs_source_data> IS ASSIGNED
                                          THEN transform_structure_data(  iv_entity_id   = is_context-entity_id
                                                                          iv_source_data = <lfs_source_data> )
                                        WHEN <lfs_t_source_data> IS ASSIGNED
                                          THEN transform_table_data(  iv_entity_id   = is_context-entity_id
                                                                      iv_source_data = <lfs_t_source_data> ) ).

    CHECK lr_transformed_data IS BOUND.

    IF ls_entity_config-src_type_structure = abap_true.
      CREATE DATA lr_data TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF (ls_entity_config-trg_type).

      ASSIGN lr_transformed_data->* TO <lfs_output_data>.
      ASSIGN lr_data->* TO <lfs_t_output_data>.

      CHECK <lfs_output_data> IS ASSIGNED AND <lfs_t_output_data> IS ASSIGNED.

      APPEND <lfs_output_data> TO  <lfs_t_output_data>.

    ELSE.
      ASSIGN lr_transformed_data->* TO <lfs_t_output_data>.
      CHECK <lfs_t_output_data> IS ASSIGNED.
    ENDIF.

    set_response_data( ii_response = ii_response
                       it_data     = <lfs_t_output_data> ).

    IF <lfs_source_data> IS  ASSIGNED.
      UNASSIGN <lfs_source_data>.
    ENDIF.
    IF <lfs_t_output_data> IS ASSIGNED.
      UNASSIGN <lfs_t_output_data>.
    ENDIF.
    IF <lfs_t_output_data> IS ASSIGNED.
      UNASSIGN <lfs_t_output_data>.
    ENDIF.
Execution inside the method:
 

Step 6 — Extract Payload Data

Source data is extracted from the payload container.

Method:

EXTRACT_DATA

Example:

    FIELD-SYMBOLS: <lfs_t_reftable> TYPE any.

    DATA(lv_type) = VALUE #( gt_entity_config[ entity_id          = iv_entity_id
                                               src_is_entity_set  = mv_main_source_entity_set ]-src_type OPTIONAL ).

    DATA(lv_entity_set) = VALUE #( gt_entity_config[  entity_id         = iv_entity_id
                                                      src_is_entity_set = mv_main_source_entity_set ]-entity_set OPTIONAL ).

    CREATE DATA rr_data TYPE (lv_type).
    ASSIGN rr_data->* TO <lfs_t_reftable>.

    IF <lfs_t_reftable> IS ASSIGNED.
      unwrap( EXPORTING it_data        = VALUE #( gt_payload[ entity_set_name = lv_entity_set ]-data OPTIONAL )
                        iv_entity_set  = lv_entity_set
              IMPORTING ev_data        = <lfs_t_reftable> ).
      UNASSIGN <lfs_t_reftable>.
    ENDIF.
 This returns a dynamic data reference.

Step 7 — Transform the Data

The framework determines whether the payload is:

  • A structure
  • A table

Structure Conversion

Method:
TRANSFORM_STRUCTURE_DATA
Example:
    FIELD-SYMBOLS: <lfs_source> TYPE any,
                   <lfs_target> TYPE any.

    DATA: lr_source_ref TYPE REF TO data,
          lr_target_ref TYPE REF TO data.

    READ TABLE gt_entity_config INTO DATA(ls_entity_cfg) WITH KEY entity_id         = iv_entity_id
                                                                  src_is_entity_set = mv_main_source_entity_set.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    CREATE DATA lr_source_ref TYPE (ls_entity_cfg-src_type)."Creation of Reference Structure for V2 Structure
    CREATE DATA lr_target_ref TYPE (ls_entity_cfg-trg_type)."Creation of Reference Structure for V4 Structure

    ASSIGN lr_source_ref->* TO <lfs_source>.
    <lfs_source> = CORRESPONDING #( iv_source_data ).

    ASSIGN lr_target_ref->* TO <lfs_target>.

    LOOP AT ls_entity_cfg-field_map ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_field_map>).
      ASSIGN COMPONENT <lfs_field_map>-src_field OF STRUCTURE <lfs_source> TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_src_val>).
      ASSIGN COMPONENT <lfs_field_map>-trg_field OF STRUCTURE <lfs_target> TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_trg_val>).

      IF <lfs_src_val> IS ASSIGNED AND <lfs_trg_val> IS ASSIGNED.
        DATA(lv_conv_val) = conversion_exit( EXPORTING iv_source     = <lfs_source>
                                                       iv_entity_id  = iv_entity_id
                                                       iv_field_name = <lfs_field_map>-trg_field ).

        <lfs_trg_val> = COND #( WHEN lv_conv_val IS NOT INITIAL THEN lv_conv_val
                               ELSE <lfs_src_val> ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    CREATE DATA rr_data LIKE <lfs_target>.
    rr_data->* = <lfs_target>.
 

Table Conversion

Method: 
TRANSFORM_TABLE_DATA​
Example:
    FIELD-SYMBOLS: <lfs_t_target_table> TYPE STANDARD TABLE,
                   <lfs_source_row>     TYPE any,
                   <lfs_target_row>     TYPE any.

    DATA: lr_source_table_ref TYPE REF TO data,
          lr_target_table_ref TYPE REF TO data.

    READ TABLE gt_entity_config INTO DATA(ls_entity_config) WITH KEY  entity_id         = iv_entity_id
                                                                      src_is_entity_set = mv_main_source_entity_set.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    CREATE DATA lr_source_table_ref TYPE (ls_entity_config-src_type)."Creation of Reference Table for V2 Structure
    CREATE DATA lr_target_table_ref TYPE (ls_entity_config-trg_type)."Creation of Reference Table for V4 Structure

    ASSIGN lr_source_table_ref->* TO <lfs_t_target_table>.
    <lfs_t_target_table> = CORRESPONDING #( iv_source_data ).

    ASSIGN lr_target_table_ref->* TO <lfs_t_target_table>.

    LOOP AT lr_source_table_ref->* ASSIGNING <lfs_source_row>.
      CREATE DATA lr_target_table_ref LIKE LINE OF <lfs_t_target_table>.
      ASSIGN lr_target_table_ref->* TO <lfs_target_row>.

      LOOP AT ls_entity_config-field_map ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_field_mapping>). "#EC CI_NESTED.
        ASSIGN COMPONENT <lfs_field_mapping>-src_field OF STRUCTURE <lfs_source_row> TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_source_value>).
        ASSIGN COMPONENT <lfs_field_mapping>-trg_field OF STRUCTURE <lfs_target_row> TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_target_value>).
        DATA(lv_converted_value) = conversion_exit( EXPORTING iv_source     = <lfs_source_row>
                                                              iv_entity_id  = iv_entity_id
                                                              iv_field_name = <lfs_field_mapping>-src_field ).

        <lfs_target_value> = COND #( WHEN lv_converted_value IS NOT INITIAL THEN lv_converted_value
                                    ELSE <lfs_source_value> ).

      ENDLOOP.

      APPEND <lfs_target_row> TO <lfs_t_target_table>.
    ENDLOOP.

    CREATE DATA rr_data LIKE <lfs_t_target_table>.
    rr_data->* = <lfs_t_target_table>.

Step 8 — Apply Custom Field Conversions

Before assigning values, the framework checks for custom conversions.

Method:

CONVERSION_EXIT
Example:
    "Redefine and write your own conversions. Example:If any logic was written in D24 for a field, you can write it here.
    CASE iv_entity_id.
      WHEN 'Entity ID 1'.
        ASSIGN COMPONENT iv_field_name OF STRUCTURE iv_source TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_source_value>).
        IF iv_field_name = 'FIELD NAME'.
          rv_field_value = 'Custom Logic' ##NO_TEXT.
        ENDIF.
      WHEN OTHERS.
    ENDCASE.

This allows transformations such as:

  • Timestamp conversion
  • Code translation
  • Business logic fields

Step 9 — Send Response Back to RAP

Finally, the transformed data is sent to the RAP response object.

Method:

SET_RESPONSE_DATA
 Example:
    ii_response->set_total_number_of_records( lines( it_data ) ).
    ii_response->set_data( it_data ).
 RAP then returns the result to the OData V4 service consumer.

 

Key Takeaway

The framework follows a layered execution approach:

 

LayerResponsibility
RAP LayerEntry point for OData V4 request
Context LayerExtract request parameters
Data LayerRetrieve V2 payload
Transformation LayerConvert V2 → V4
Response LayerReturn RAP response
 
This design ensures the framework is:
  • Reusable
  • Extensible
  • Entity independent
  • Compatible with RAP architecture

entry.title: Custom Business Configurations (F4579): Collaborative draft
entry.content:
Introduction

The Custom Business Configurations (CUBCO) app serves as an entry point to the Business Configuration Maintenance Object (SMBC) provided by custom applications or partners.

When you use the ADT wizard to generate a Business Configuration Maintenance Object, the singleton pattern is used when generating the draft enabled RAP BO. One characteristic of this pattern is that all child entities are locked together, so it is not possible for multiple users to work with the same configuration at the same time.

This blog describes how to avoid this restriction by using collaborative draft.

This blog is relevant for

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (2025)
  • SAP BTP ABAP environment 
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 

Further reading:

  • Related blog posts
  • Learn how you can use ABAP technology to develop innovative applications and business solutions across SAP’s portfolio on SAP Learning Site.

Example scenario

In the configuration table ZDEMO_CC, the company codes are stored.

@EndUserText.label : 'Company Code'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zdemo_cc {
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key company_code      : abap.numc(4) not null;
  country               : land1;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
}

Enable collaborative draft

In the generated behavior definition, add "collaborative" to the draft annotation:

managed with additional save implementation in class ZBP_I_COMPANYCODE_S unique;
strict;
with collaborative draft;

Add the draft query annotation and the share action to the singleton entity definition:

define behavior for ZI_CompanyCode_S alias CompanyCodeAll
draft table ZDEMO_CC_D_S query ZR_DEMO_CC_CD
[...]
{
  draft action Share;
  [...]
}

 Right-click the singleton draft table and choose New Data Definition to create the CDS view for the draft query:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Draft Query View'
define view entity ZR_DEMO_CC_CD
  as select from zdemo_cc_d_s
{
  key singletonid                   as Singletonid,
      lastchangedatmax              as Lastchangedatmax,
      transportrequestid            as Transportrequestid,
      draftentitycreationdatetime   as Draftentitycreationdatetime,
      draftentitylastchangedatetime as Draftentitylastchangedatetime,
      draftadministrativedatauuid   as Draftadministrativedatauuid,
      draftentityoperationcode      as Draftentityoperationcode,
      hasactiveentity               as Hasactiveentity,
      draftfieldchanges             as Draftfieldchanges
}

You need to enhance the authorization of the involved users. In SAP BTP ABAP environment and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition you assign business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_RAP_DRAFT_PC to the user, in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition authorization object S_DRAFT.

Collaboration

User TB is creating a new company code 400 while at the same time user PW is editing company code 100. All changes are recorded on the same transport request.

Since the collaborative draft is enabled for the singleton root entity, the save and discard draft action are applied to all object changes. In this example, users PW and TB process two different company codes, which are logically different objects. Technically, however, they process the same singleton root entity. In practice, this means that discarding the draft or saving must be aligned between the users involved, as the save action persists the changes in all company codes.
For completely independent editing of individual objects, you must develop a RAP BO without a singleton pattern.

entry.title: Transaction cannot be revived after delayed abortion(RAP_RUNTIME 019) - Must ROLLBACK After Failures
entry.content:
1. Symptom (ST22 / Dump Keywords)

A typical dump looks like this (same category as in your case):

  • Runtime Error: RAISE_SHORTDUMP
  • Exception: CX_SADL_DUMP_APPL_MODEL_ERROR
  • T100: RAP_RUNTIME / 019
  • Short Text: Transaction cannot be revived after delayed abortion (BO: I_ENTERPRISEPROJECTTP_2)

When debugging you may also notice RAP internal state like:

  • CL_RAP_BHV_PROCESSOR->IF_RAP_LEGACY_TRANSACTION~MV_ABORT = abap_true

Meaning: RAP has already marked the logical transaction as aborted (delayed abortion). It must not be reused.

2. Root Cause: RAP Transaction State + Delayed Abortion

A RAP EML “logical transaction” typically consists of two phases:

  1. Transaction phase
  • MODIFY ENTITIES runs validations/determinations/checks.
  • Errors are usually returned in FAILED / REPORTED
  1. Save phase
  • COMMIT ENTITIES triggers the save sequence (finalize/save, etc.).
  • Errors again come back via FAILED / REPORTED (in the commit response this is the LATE response).

If anything fails in either phase, RAP can enter delayed abortion:
Your ABAP code continues running,but the RAP logical transaction becomes unusable.
Any subsequent EML using that aborted transaction context may lead to RAP_RUNTIME 019.

MODIFY ENTITIES OF <BO> ... FAILED ls_failed_tx REPORTED ls_reported_tx.
COMMIT ENTITIES ... FAILED ls_failed_save REPORTED ls_reported_save.

" If commit failed but you don't rollback here,
" next EML call can dump (RAP_RUNTIME 019)
MODIFY ENTITIES OF <BO> ...

Key point: Never continue with another EML step after a failure without rollback.

5. Correct Example Using I_EnterpriseProjectTP_3

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report zdemo_rap_eml_epp3
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
REPORT zdemo_rap_eml_epp3.

PARAMETERS:
      p_uuid TYPE sysuuid_c36 OBLIGATORY,
      p_desc TYPE string      DEFAULT 'Demo update from EML'.

START-OF-SELECTION.

  DATA lv_uuid_x16 TYPE sysuuid_x16.

  TRY.
              cl_system_uuid=>convert_uuid_c36_static(
                  EXPORTING
                      uuid     = p_uuid
                  IMPORTING
                      uuid_x16 = lv_uuid_x16 ).
        CATCH cx_uuid_error.
              WRITE: / 'Invalid UUID format. Use 36-char UUID (xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx).'.
              RETURN.
  ENDTRY.

  DATA:
        ls_failed_tx     TYPE RESPONSE FOR FAILED   i_enterpriseprojecttp_3,
        ls_reported_tx   TYPE RESPONSE FOR REPORTED i_enterpriseprojecttp_3,
        ls_failed_save   TYPE RESPONSE FOR FAILED   LATE i_enterpriseprojecttp_3,
        ls_reported_save TYPE RESPONSE FOR REPORTED LATE i_enterpriseprojecttp_3.

  TRY.
              " Transaction phase
              MODIFY ENTITIES OF i_enterpriseprojecttp_3
                  ENTITY EnterpriseProject
                  UPDATE FIELDS ( ProjectDescription )
                  WITH VALUE #(
                      ( %key-ProjectUUID      = lv_uuid_x16
                          ProjectDescription    = p_desc )
                  )
                  FAILED   ls_failed_tx
                  REPORTED ls_reported_tx.

              IF ls_failed_tx IS NOT INITIAL.
                    WRITE: / 'MODIFY ENTITIES failed (transaction phase) -> ROLLBACK ENTITIES'.
                    ROLLBACK ENTITIES.
                    RETURN.
              ENDIF.

              " Save phase
              COMMIT ENTITIES
                  BEGIN RESPONSE OF i_enterpriseprojecttp_3
                  FAILED   ls_failed_save
                  REPORTED ls_reported_save.
              COMMIT ENTITIES END.

              IF ls_failed_save IS NOT INITIAL.
                    WRITE: / 'COMMIT ENTITIES failed (save phase) -> ROLLBACK ENTITIES'.
                    ROLLBACK ENTITIES.
                    RETURN.
              ENDIF.

              WRITE: / 'COMMIT ENTITIES OK.'.

        CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_root).
              WRITE: / 'Unexpected exception:', lx_root->get_text( ).
              " Keep the EML contract: if anything goes wrong, end the logical transaction.
              ROLLBACK ENTITIES.
  ENDTRY.

Two important details in this example

  1. COMMIT ENTITIES requires LATE response types
  • RESPONSE FOR FAILED LATE i_enterpriseprojecttp_3
  • RESPONSE FOR REPORTED LATE i_enterpriseprojecttp_3
  1. “Fail fast” with rollback
  • If FAILED is not initial in either phase, rollback and end the logical transaction.
  • Start the next business step in a new logical transaction context.

 

8. FAQ: Why not ROLLBACK WORK?

In RAP EML, use the EML-compatible statement:

  • Errors in EML logical transaction → ROLLBACK ENTITIES

ROLLBACK WORK is lower-level LUW handling and can lead to semantic mismatches with RAP’s logical transaction handling. The dump you saw is fundamentally about the RAP logical transaction being aborted and still being reused.

entry.title: CAP, RAP, or Both: Stop Hiding Behind 'It Depends'
entry.content:
I've read every major article on this topic. They all end the same way:

"Both models are complementary. Choose based on your context. Use each for the right scenario."

That is a technically accurate, professionally safe, and completely useless answer.

This article gives you the framework I actually use with clients — including the uncomfortable parts most posts skip: the staffing bias that impact architecture decisions, what to do when your client has thousands of Z-objects, why migrating RAP to CAP is not a refactor, and why CAP is the only model that enforces the Clean Core promise architecturally.

One thing upfront: this is not an anti-ABAP article. ABAP knowledge — deep understanding of business processes, transactional models, and SAP data structures accumulated over years — is one of the most valuable assets in enterprise

What We're Actually Debating

RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) is SAP's extension model for the S/4HANA core and ABAP Cloud environments. It's ABAP-native, optimized for extending standard SAP Business Objects with transactional consistency. SAP's answer to "how do I extend S/4HANA without modifying standard code."

CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) is SAP's framework for building cloud-native services on SAP BTP. Node.js and Java, BTP Cloud Foundry and Kyma, multitenancy, eventing, and OData/GraphQL out of the box. SAP's answer to "how do I build new business capabilities that live outside the S/4HANA core."

Same goal — Clean Core, BTP-native development. Fundamentally different execution contexts.

The Uncomfortable Truth First

Most teams choosing between CAP and RAP are not making a pure architectural decision.

They're making a decision shaped by team composition.

An ABAP-heavy organization will present RAP as the natural choice for almost any BTP extension. The justification will sound architectural: "We need access to S/4HANA data," "We want to leverage existing ABAP logic," "RAP is Clean Core approved." All of those statements can be true and still be a rationalization. The real driver is often simpler: the team knows ABAP, so RAP wins.

This is a systemic pattern worth naming explicitly — because when staffing drives architecture, the decisions look reasonable one at a time and catastrophic in aggregate. The job of a principal architect is to separate these two questions before the decision is made.

When RAP Is Genuinely the Right Answer

1. Extending Standard SAP Business Objects

If you need to extend standard S/4HANA entities — Sales Orders, Purchase Requisitions, Business Partners, Material Documents — RAP is purpose-built for this. Proper lock handling, transactional consistency, draft management, clean integration with standard SAP workflows. Trying to replicate this via CAP calling S/4HANA APIs introduces latency, complexity, and consistency gaps that are difficult to manage at scale. For tight S/4HANA core extensions, RAP's native access is a genuine advantage, not just familiarity.

2. On-Premise + Cloud Parity Requirements

RAP runs on both on-premise ABAP Platform and ABAP Cloud. CAP is cloud-only. Hybrid deployment, phased cloud migration, regulated industries with on-premise constraints — RAP is the only viable option when parity is required.

3. Maximizing Existing ABAP Investment

Complex, well-tested ABAP business logic that needs to be exposed as a service can be wrapped with a Clean Core API facade in RAP without rewriting it. Legitimate — with the caveat covered in the Z section below.

4. Simple, Bounded In-App Extensions

A single-source, single-consumer extension with no external integrations and no scalability concerns does not need the infrastructure overhead of CAP. Don't over-engineer.

When CAP Is the Right Answer — Which Is More Often Than You Think

1. Multi-Source Data Scenarios

The moment your extension needs data from more than one system, CAP is the correct choice. CAP's service layer was designed for data federation. RAP operating across multiple external sources becomes a manual integration exercise.

2. Independent Scalability

Side-by-side BTP extensions with variable load profiles should not be constrained by ABAP runtime characteristics. CAP on BTP Cloud Foundry or Kyma scales horizontally.

3. Talent Pool and Long-Term Maintainability

The global Node.js and Java developer pool is orders of magnitude larger than ABAP Cloud specialists. But the real point is different: the scarce resource in SAP programs is not syntax knowledge — it's SAP business process knowledge. An architecture that requires ABAP Cloud specialists to maintain it creates organizational dependency risk. An ABAP architect who already has the process knowledge and adds CAP to their toolkit is the most valuable person in the room.

4. Multi-Tenant SaaS and Reusable Services

CAP's multitenancy model is production-grade out of the box. RAP was not designed for this.

5. Non-SAP Ecosystem Integration

When your extension lives at the intersection of SAP and non-SAP systems, CAP's polyglot nature is a structural advantage. Building this in RAP means engineering brittle ABAP-to-external-API bridges.

6. Survival of SAP Upgrade Cycles

CAP extensions communicate with S/4HANA via stable, versioned APIs. They have no ABAP lifecycle. This connects directly to the Clean Core argument below.

The Big Z Problem

Most clients don't arrive at this debate with a clean slate. They arrive with 1,000, 2,000, sometimes 5,000 Z-objects accumulated over decades of ECC customization. The first proposal is almost always: "Let's migrate the Zs to RAP."

This is the wrong answer — and it is expensive.

The right question, before any migration decision, is: why does each Z-object exist?

Category 1 — SAP now covers it natively (retire it)
A significant portion of Z-objects from ECC exist because SAP didn't have that functionality in 2005. S/4HANA and BTP may have it natively today. Before migrating anything, check whether SAP standard now covers the need. If it does: retire the Z. Don't migrate what you should delete. 

Category 2 — Genuine core business object extension (evaluate RAP)
Some Z-objects encode real business logic tightly coupled to SAP transactional objects — custom validations on Sales Orders, derivations on Financial documents, extensions to MM flows. These are legitimate RAP candidates because the coupling is intentional and the transactional integrity requirement is real.

Category 3 — Side-by-side logic that never belonged in the core (evaluate CAP with bias)
Many Z-objects are reporting utilities, data extraction routines, integration helpers, or workflow logic placed inside the ABAP stack out of convenience, not architecture. These should be evaluated for CAP first — finally living where they should have lived from the beginning. One qualifier: if the logic requires HANA-native pushdown on large datasets (financial close, ATP, margin analysis at scale), or participates in SAP's transactional determination chain, evaluate before rebuilding. The default is still CAP. The exception needs justification.

The Big Z is not a RAP migration backlog. It's a forcing function to finally answer which of your customizations your business actually needs, which SAP now covers natively, and which were architectural accidents waiting to be corrected.

And for the Zs that survive the triage but can't be rewritten this quarter — wrap them. A clean API boundary around legacy logic is not technical debt; it's the first act of modernization. What you cannot afford is leaving Z-code directly exposed. That's how "temporary" becomes permanent.

The CAP First Decision Protocol

CAP First means the burden of proof shifts. The default is CAP. RAP must earn its place by satisfying a specific gate.

The only gate that stops CAP: transactional consistency with a standard SAP Business Object, bounded to S/4HANA data, with no external systems, no multi-tenancy, and no independent scalability requirements.

One honest prerequisite: CAP First is the right posture for organizations that have already invested in BTP platform maturity — an operational subaccount, CI/CD for Node.js or Java, and the operational capability for Cloud Foundry or Kyma workloads. For organizations that haven't built that foundation yet, CAP First is still the right destination — it comes with a deliberate enablement journey first. The posture doesn't change. The timeline does.

Why does the default posture matter? Because the cost of the wrong decision is asymmetric.

The Real Cost of Getting the Model Wrong

A clarification worth making explicitly, because ABAP architects will correctly challenge a weaker version of this argument:

Upgrading S/4HANA with existing RAP extensions is relatively low cost — if your RAP uses only ABAP Cloud Released APIs (C1), which is what Clean Core RAP should do. SAP guarantees stability on released APIs across versions. ABAP teams who make this point are correct.

The cost argument I'm making is different. It's about switching models — what happens when you built in RAP and later discover that CAP was the right choice: multi-source data appeared, scalability became a requirement, the business needs this as a multi-tenant service.

RAP is ABAP. CAP is Node.js or Java. These are not different versions of the same language — different runtimes, different deployment models, different ecosystems entirely. When you migrate a RAP service to CAP:

  • The CDS data model needs to be rewritten in CAP CDS (similar syntax, different runtime behavior)
  • All business logic needs to be reimplemented in Node.js or Java — not ported, reimplemented
  • The deployment pipeline, CI/CD, monitoring, and operational model all change
  • The ABAP artifacts are retired, not converted

For a medium-complexity service: weeks to months. For a large one: a quarter or more. For a portfolio that grew because nobody pushed back: a program, not a project.

The cost of the wrong default is asymmetric. Not because RAP upgrades are expensive — they're not. But because choosing the wrong model and needing to switch is. CAP First reduces the risk of landing on that side.

One variable that changes this equation: AI. Claude Code connected to SAP ADT via MCP can analyze RAP logic and generate CAP equivalents faster than before — it lowers the code generation cost. It does not replace the authorization model revalidation, the test suite rebuild, or the operational cutover, which is where most of the real effort lives. The fear factor drops. The effort doesn't disappear. That full conversation is a follow-up article.

CAP Enforces the Clean Core Promise. RAP Requires Discipline to Maintain It.

SAP's official definition of Clean Core: don't modify standard code, use the approved extensibility models. RAP is on that list — and a RAP extension using only ABAP Cloud Released APIs (C1) is genuinely Clean Core compliant by SAP's own definition. Let's be precise about this.

But Clean Core has a purpose beyond compliance: the ability to upgrade your SAP core without touching your business logic. Faster upgrades. Lower risk. Smaller impact windows.

Here is the real distinction between RAP and CAP on this point — and it is not about compliance. It is about enforcement.

Both models can satisfy the Clean Core compliance definition when implemented with discipline. The difference is what happens when discipline breaks down. In RAP, a team under delivery pressure can find paths to unreleased ABAP internals — friend class patterns, dynamic calls, legacy function module invocations. The architectural boundary exists as a rule, not a wall. In CAP, you are physically outside the ABAP stack. You cannot access unreleased internals because there is no path to them from a Node.js or Java runtime. The boundary is enforced by the architecture itself.

RAP requires discipline to stay Clean Core. CAP makes it structurally difficult to leave.

This is not a criticism of ABAP developers. It is a recognition that architectural guardrails matter most under pressure — and pressure is the normal operating condition of enterprise SAP programs.

A valid counterpoint here: ABAP Cloud on BTP — the ABAP Environment as a side-by-side deployment — does break the S/4HANA lifecycle dependency. The toolchain enforces Released APIs syntactically via the language version check, so the "discipline" argument partially weakens. But there is a distinction worth making: ABAP Cloud on BTP decouples you from S/4HANA's runtime lifecycle. CAP decouples you from the ABAP architecture itself. If your constraints are about upgrade survival, both work. If your constraints are about talent pool, integration patterns, and long-term architectural independence away from the ABAP stack — only one of them gets you there.

A CAP service communicates with S/4HANA through stable, versioned OData or API endpoints. It does not know what version of S/4HANA is running underneath. It has no ABAP lifecycle. It can be upgraded, redeployed, and scaled completely independently. RAP is the right direction. CAP is the destination. The difference is whether Clean Core survives contact with reality.

This Is Not Anti-ABAP. It's a Call to Deploy ABAP Knowledge Differently.

Deep ABAP expertise — understanding transactional models, business process flows, SAP data structures built over decades — is irreplaceable. The scarce resource in SAP programs is not syntax knowledge. It is SAP business process knowledge. A Node.js developer learns the CAP framework in weeks. They do not learn how SAP processes an intercompany stock transfer in weeks. ABAP architects already have the hard part.

The problem is not ABAP knowledge. The problem is when that knowledge becomes a reason to avoid CAP rather than a foundation for it.

In 2026, with Claude Code connected to SAP ADT via MCP and Joule for Developers embedded in the SAP ecosystem, an experienced ABAP developer can build a production-grade CAP service with AI assistance in a fraction of the time it would have taken two years ago. The language gap has never been smaller to bridge. The "we don't have CAP developers" argument has never been weaker.

And the most mature SAP architectures don't choose one model. They use both deliberately: RAP as the API layer of S/4HANA — the clean, stable interface that exposes core business objects. CAP as the orchestration and composition layer above it — multi-source, scalable, independently deployable. That is not a compromise. That is the target state. ABAP architects who understand both are positioned to design it.

A Challenge for Your Next Architecture Review

Before your team decides on RAP — or commits to a Z-to-RAP migration program — answer four questions publicly in the room:

  1. For Z-objects: Does each Z exist because SAP lacked functionality that it now has natively? If yes: retire it, don't migrate it.
  2. For new extensions: Does this require transactional consistency with a standard SAP Business Object, bounded to S/4HANA data only? If yes: RAP is correct.
  3. For new extensions: Does this require data from outside S/4HANA, independent scalability, or multi-tenant deployment? If yes: CAP is correct.
  4. Before committing to either: Is RAP on the table because of architectural justification — or because of who is in the room?

Question 4 is the one that changes the outcome. And it takes organizational courage to ask it out loud.

The CAP vs. RAP debate is not a technology debate. It's an organizational clarity debate — and more often than not, an organizational courage debate. The teams that get this right are the ones willing to name what's actually driving the decision, separate ABAP expertise from ABAP default, and deploy both models where they genuinely belong.

References

  • RAP vs CAP - Key Differences between the Two Programming Models — SAP Community
  • CAP vs. RAP: Navigating the Choice of SAP Programming Models — SAP Community
  • ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model — SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Cloud Application Programming Model — SAP Community
  • Five Reasons to Use CAP — qmacro.org
  • Clean Core Extensibility: Balancing Standardization and Differentiation — SAP Community

Walter Aguero Machado —SAP Enterprise Architect | SAP BTP SME at Globant | SAP Certified Solution Architect & BTP Administrator | ASUG Speaker

entry.title: XML Annotations: The Essentials Before Adapting Documentation Samples to Your Project
entry.content:
The Problem

When building Fiori Elements apps, you sometimes need to add XML annotations on the UI side. The usual approach is to search for examples in the UI5 Demo Kit. For instance, you might find something like this:

Disabling the Search filter

<Annotations Target="SAP__self.Container/SalesOrder">
   <Annotation Term="SAP__capabilities.SearchRestrictions">
      <Record>
         <PropertyValue Property="Searchable" Bool="false" />
      </Record>
   </Annotation>
</Annotations>

However, simply copying this into your annotation.xml won't work. To figure out what needs to be changed, you need to understand the basic structure of annotations.

 

What You Need to Know

1. Annotation Targets

The target of an annotation varies depending on the annotation you want to apply. Here are the most common ones:

TargetSyntax for Target attributeExample in UI5 Documentation
EntityType{Namespace}.{EntityType}Common for UI annotations (UI.FieldGroup, UI.HeaderInfo, etc.). Note: documentation examples often show only the inner <Annotation> element, omitting the outer <Annotations Target="..."> wrapper.
EntitySet{Namespace}.{EntityContainer}/{EntitySet}TravelService.EntityContainer/Travel [1]
Property{Namespace}.{EntityType}/{Property}Z0020_CDS.Z0020Type/CountryID [3]
NavigationProperty{Namespace}.{EntityContainer}/{EntitySet}/{NavigationProperty}myService.EntityContainer/SalesOrderManage/_Item [2]
Action (Bound){Namespace}.{Action}({BindingParameterType})com.c_salesordermanage_sd.CreateWithSalesOrderType(com.c_salesordermanage_sd.SalesOrderManage) [2]
Action (Unbound){Namespace}.{Action}SAP__self.CreateWithSalesOrderType [2]
Action Parameter{Namespace}.{Action}/{Parameter}SalesOrder.CreateWithSalesOrderType/SalesOrganization [2]
EntityContainer{Namespace}.{EntityContainer}namespace.EntityContainer [4]

[1] Disabling the Editing Status Filter
[2] Actions
[3] In / Out Mappings in the ValueList Annotation
[4] Adapting the Filter Bar
